text: with several circles dotted around them. “I’ve been watching you for some time,” he says. “I come in for a drink now and then on my way to other destinations. Usually I sit at the other end of the bar. I’ve noticed that you also like to watch the community here pinball around each other, preferring to stay in the shadows, basking in whatever energy they give off.” I shrug my shoulders, and take a sip of my drink. “I’ve also noticed that every now and then one of the inebriated young ladies will approach you, curious as to what presence is giving off such dark vibes from the depths of this seedy bar. No offense intended.” “None taken.” “Usually they move on, and I go back to my drink, relieved. But sometimes they go home with you. And then I never see them again.” I sigh. “I don’t kill them, if that’s what you’re asking.” He grins at me, a mouth full of teeth that is far too wide and full for my taste. “I thought that for a while,” he says, “and then I ran into this boisterous redhead down the street at another establishment, fit as a fiddle. Though every now and then she did seem a bit agitated. She kept looking over her shoulder, toward the door, usually, as if she was expecting someone. And then she went back to her festivities. Bent, but not broken, right?” “I don’t know about that.” “Oh, but you do,” he continued. “And that’s why I’m here. I can tell that you’re suffering. And that you have been for some time. I think I can help you.” I take a deep breath, and exhale. I’ve been here before. Seen therapists, gone to counselors, taken drugs—nothing worked. I know what I did, my role in the tragedies that happened. There are no excuses, and I will never forgive myself. “I have a business proposition for you. Come to my office whenever you have a spare moment. I know that you don’t care about yourself…” he says, picking up the bottle, and filling my glass, “…and I’m not here to stop you from this slow decline into madness and delusion. But you may be able to help others.” He pauses for a moment, his eyes washing over me, and I shiver again, as if a thousand spiders are traversing my flesh, seeking entry. And then he continues. “And that may be the best way to exorcise your demons.” He picks up his drink, extracts the three olives with his other hand, opens his cavernous mouth, and pours the entire martini down his gullet. Grinning, he sets the glass down, and then places the skewer between his teeth, and pulls the olives off, one at a time, chewing slowly, as he closes his eyes in a silent rapture. It unsettles me to no end. “Come by, we’ll talk. I can’t sit in your presence any longer,” he says, standing up, brushing off his suit as if it is covered in dandruff and dog hair. “It’s quite odious, and I feel rather sick.” I open my mouth to say something but he silences me with his hand. “But that’s what I’m looking for. This is who you are.” I close my eyes for a second, and when I open them again, his long, slender legs have skittered across the bar, where he pauses at the door and glances back. He winks, and disappears into the night, his shadow holding the frame for a moment, then departing with him, out into the cold. I tear up the business card and burn it in the fading candle that sits in the center of the table. # When I wake up the next day, at home in my one-bedroom apartment, the darkness and shadows wrap around me like a blanket. There is the sound of a ticking clock, the humming of the refrigerator, and the flush of a toilet from the neighbors above me. Muttering drifts to me, and then several thuds, followed by the scraping of metal, the sound of something cooking in a pan, smoky and meaty, crackling into the air. Outside, something that might be rain, or even snow, shushes and spits, so I burrow under the blankets, eager to hibernate as long as I can. When I turn my head to the nightstand, the business card sits there, intact. Deep inside my empty ribcage there is a long slow moan building, an echo that feels like a scream muffled by rough hands and a thick pillow, my skin suddenly flushed with a slowly building sweat that threatens to spill across my flesh. I throw the card away, and it reappears the next day. I cut it into tiny pieces, and place the bits in three different garbage cans, including the dumpster out back. It’s on the kitchen counter, leaning against the coffee machine, the following day. I drown it in the sink until it separates and dissolves, only to find it in the medicine cabinet soon after. I give up and go to see the man. # In the shadows, there is giggling, a click, and the sound of a heavy metal door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away, fading down the hallway. Later there is a loud noise, a bang, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh, followed by gentle sobbing. # The address is in a nondescript strip mall between a tobacco shop and a questionable massage parlor. There is no sign, no neon, no name—just an address, and a solid door made out what looks like steel, with a heavy wrought iron handle, and rivets running up and down the seams. It is formidable. As I pull it open, I ask myself if it’s here to keep people in, or out? As I walk into the main room, the only space that I can see, there are several chairs
